events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African descent, a retired schoolteacher, nonsmoker, with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (10 years, on metformin) and hypertension. BMI 24.1. No family history of pancreatic cancer. She lives alone but has strong family support.

At age 66 years, the patient was diagnosed with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), stage III (AJCC 8th edition, T4 N1 M0). Primary tumor site: head and uncinate process of the pancreas (ICD-O-3 site code C25.0; histology code 8500/3, infiltrating duct carcinoma NOS). Summary stage: Regional. Tumor measured 3.8 cm in greatest dimension on CT. The tumor abutted the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over 180 degrees of circumference and was in direct contact with the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with partial occlusion. One regional lymph node was involved (peripancreatic). CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 1,247 U/mL (markedly elevated). CEA: 8.4 ng/mL. Confirmation: Positive histology by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy. No distant metastases identified. Performance status ECOG 1. New-onset diabetes worsening noted in preceding 3 months, prompting workup.

At age 66 years, the patient underwent EUS-guided fine needle biopsy of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology showed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (infiltrating duct carcinoma, grade 2). Tumor cells showed prominent desmoplastic stroma. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative, SMAD4 (DPC4) loss of expression by IHC, consistent with homozygous deletion or inactivating mutation. Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 35%. No perineural invasion assessable on this biopsy specimen. Findings confirmed primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

At age 66 years, the patient had a multiphasic CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast (pancreatic protocol). Findings: Hypoenhancing mass in the head/uncinate process of the pancreas measuring 3.8 x 3.2 cm. Tumor contact with the SMA greater than 180 degrees. Direct abutment and partial encasement of the SMV with mild luminal narrowing but no complete occlusion. Moderate dilation of the main pancreatic duct (6 mm) and mild intrahepatic biliary dilation with CBD diameter 12 mm. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. One peripancreatic lymph node 1.2 cm suspicious for involvement. No distant organ metastases. Impression: Borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV involvement render immediate resection unfeasible.

At age 66 years, the patient had a PET-CT (FDG) scan for staging. Findings: Intense FDG-avid mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate (SUVmax 8.9). One FDG-avid peripancreatic lymph node (SUVmax 4.2). No hepatic FDG-avid lesions. No pulmonary nodules with uptake. No peritoneal FDG avidity. No bone lesions. Impression: FDG-avid primary pancreatic mass with regional nodal involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Consistent with locoregional pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

At age 66 years, next generation sequencing was performed on the EUS-guided biopsy specimen from the pancreatic head mass (tissue-based comprehensive genomic profiling, 324-gene panel). Actionable and clinically relevant alterations: KRAS p.G12V mutation (VAF 42%, pathogenic, oncogenic driver), TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 38%, pathogenic), SMAD4 homozygous deletion (copy number 0, consistent with IHC finding), CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Additional alterations: ARID1A p.R1276* nonsense mutation (VAF 12%), RNF43 p.G659fs frameshift mutation (VAF 8%), ATM p.V2716fs frameshift mutation (VAF 6%, germline vs somatic uncertain - germline testing recommended), BRCA2 splice site variant c.8331+1G>A (VAF 5%, low VAF - likely subclonal or technical artifact; germline testing recommended). TMB: 3 mutations/Mb (low). MSI status: MSI-stable (MSS). PD-L1 CPS: 2 (low). No NTRK fusions. No FGFR alterations. No HER2 amplification. No ALK or RET fusions. Copy number alterations: SMAD4 deletion, CDKN2A deletion, MYC gain (low level). Tumor mutational burden: Low. Note: ATM frameshift variant of uncertain germline significance prompts germline testing.

At age 66 years, the patient was evaluated by the multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board. Case reviewed by surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, radiology, and pathology. Consensus: Borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma due to SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV involvement. Recommendation for neoadjuvant systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) given good performance status (ECOG 1), adequate hepatic function, and absence of distant metastases. Plan for biliary decompression if bilirubin rises. Genetic counseling referral placed given ATM and low-VAF BRCA2 variants. Diabetes management optimization with endocrinology. Baseline CA 19-9: 1,247 U/mL. Restaging planned after 4 cycles. Cancer present. No prior therapy.

At age 66 years, the patient initiated neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX): oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced from 180 mg/m2 given age and diabetes), leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, omitting bolus 5-FU. Cycle 1 of planned 8 cycles. Prophylactic pegfilgrastim administered. Antiemetics: ondansetron, dexamethasone, olanzapine. Treatment intent: neoadjuvant, curative intent.

At age 66 years, after cycle 2 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral distal hand and foot paresthesias, oxaliplatin-related), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 2 diarrhea. Loperamide initiated for diarrhea. Cold sensitivity noted. Dose modification: oxaliplatin reduced by 20% (to 68 mg/m2) for subsequent cycles. Irinotecan maintained. No neutropenic fever. CA 19-9 after cycle 2: 620 U/mL (declining from baseline 1,247).

At age 66 years (approximately 3 months after diagnosis), the patient had a restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. Findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measuring 3.1 x 2.5 cm (previously 3.8 x 3.2 cm), representing partial response per RECIST 1.1 (approximately 22% decrease in sum of longest diameters). Improved desmoplastic halo around the tumor. SMA contact decreased to approximately 90-120 degrees of circumference. SMV contact with residual soft tissue but improved luminal caliber. Peripancreatic lymph node decreased to 0.7 cm, no longer clearly enlarged. No new lesions. Main pancreatic duct dilation improved (4 mm). Impression: Partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Tumor appears more favorably positioned relative to SMA. Continue neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 66 years, restaging clinical assessment after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. CA 19-9 declined from 1,247 to 310 U/mL (75% decline). Radiographic partial response confirmed. Patient ECOG performance status 1. Peripheral neuropathy grade 2 stable. No evidence of disease progression. Plan: Continue mFOLFIRINOX for 4 additional cycles (cycles 5-8) with oxaliplatin dose reduction in place. Reassess for surgical candidacy after completion of 8 cycles. Genetic counseling completed: germline testing ordered; ATM p.V2716fs confirmed germline pathogenic variant (heterozygous) - patient counseled on implications. BRCA2 variant not detected on germline testing (somatic only, subclonal). Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66 years, during cycles 5-8 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 3 fatigue requiring brief dose delay of 1 week after cycle 6. Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy persisted (stable). Grade 1 alopecia. Grade 1 mucositis managed with magic mouthwash. No grade 3-4 hematologic toxicity. No dose reduction beyond the prior oxaliplatin reduction was required. Patient completed all 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX with cumulative dose modifications.

At age 66 years (approximately 5 months after diagnosis), the patient had restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis and CT chest after completing 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. Findings: Pancreatic head mass measuring 2.6 x 2.1 cm (previously 3.1 x 2.5 cm at mid-treatment scan). Continued partial response. SMA contact now approximately 90 degrees or less of circumference. SMV shows residual soft tissue contact but vessel appears patent with no significant luminal compromise. No enlarged lymph nodes (largest 0.5 cm). No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal disease. No pulmonary nodules. Impression: Continued partial response after 8 cycles of neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX. Tumor response and vascular anatomy now potentially favorable for resection after consolidative chemoradiation. Tumor remains in contact with SMA; multidisciplinary reassessment for resectability recommended.

At age 66 years, multidisciplinary tumor board reassessment after 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. CA 19-9 now 85 U/mL (93% decline from baseline of 1,247 U/mL). Radiographic partial response with tumor size reduction from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm. SMA contact now approximately 90 degrees. Surgical team assessment: Patient potentially resectable but SMA contact remains borderline. Recommendation: Proceed with consolidative chemoradiation (long-course radiation with concurrent capecitabine) prior to surgical reassessment. ECOG performance status 1. Peripheral neuropathy grade 1-2 (improved). Plan for stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) vs. conventionally fractionated chemoRT discussed; consensus favored conventional long-course chemoRT with capecitabine for maximal local control. Cancer present, responding to neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 66 years (approximately 6 months after diagnosis), the patient underwent conventionally fractionated external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) to the pancreatic primary tumor and regional lymphatics. Technique: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) with daily image guidance (IGRT). Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction, 5 days/week over 5.5 weeks). Target volumes: primary gross tumor volume (GTV) encompassing pancreatic head mass and involved vascular margin, clinical target volume (CTV) including peripancreatic lymphatics and celiac axis nodal region. Concurrent radiosensitizer: capecitabine 825 mg/m2 twice daily on days of radiation. Treatment intent: consolidative neoadjuvant, curative intent. Fiducial markers placed for image guidance prior to simulation. Dosimetric constraints respected for duodenum, small bowel, stomach, liver, kidneys, and spinal cord.

At age 66 years, during chemoradiation, the patient developed grade 2 nausea and grade 2 fatigue, managed with ondansetron and dietary modifications. Grade 2 radiation-induced gastritis (epigastric pain, managed with proton pump inhibitor escalation). Grade 1 skin reaction at radiation field. Hand-foot syndrome grade 1 from capecitabine (bilateral plantar erythema, mild). No treatment interruptions required. Weight loss of 3 kg over the chemoradiation course. Nutritional counseling provided; oral supplementation initiated.

At age 66 years (approximately 2 months after completing chemoradiation, 8 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a restaging multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis and CT chest prior to surgical reassessment. Findings: Pancreatic head mass measuring 2.3 x 1.9 cm (continued reduction from 2.6 x 2.1 cm). Tumor margin adjacent to SMA with contact now estimated at approximately 60-70 degrees of circumference. SMV appears patent with only minimal soft tissue thickening at prior contact site. No new hepatic lesions. No enlarged lymph nodes. No peritoneal disease. No pulmonary metastases. CA 19-9: 72 U/mL. Impression: Further radiographic response after chemoradiation. Vascular anatomy now appears potentially favorable for R0 resection. Surgical evaluation for pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) recommended.

At age 66 years, pre-surgical assessment for pancreaticoduodenectomy. Multidisciplinary tumor board confirmed resectability based on radiographic response and CA 19-9 normalization trend. Surgical oncology: Technically resectable with intent for R0 (negative margin) resection. Anticipated need for SMV reconstruction not required based on imaging. Pre-operative optimization: HbA1c 7.8% (improved diabetes control). Nutritional status: mild pre-operative malnutrition, supplemental oral nutrition. Cardiopulmonary clearance obtained. Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation completed. Planned operation: classic pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with pylorus-preserving technique. ECOG 1. Cancer present, intent curative resection.

At age 66 years (approximately 9 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD, Whipple procedure) with curative intent. Procedure performed by hepatobiliary surgical oncologist. Intraoperative findings: Primary tumor in pancreatic head/uncinate process, grossly adherent to but separable from the SMA. SMV was dissected free without need for venous reconstruction. No peritoneal implants identified. No liver metastases on intraoperative inspection. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin and common bile duct margin were negative for malignancy. Procedure: en bloc resection of pancreatic head, uncinate process, duodenum, proximal 15 cm jejunum, gallbladder, distal common bile duct, and distal stomach remnant. Regional lymphadenectomy performed. Reconstruction: pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa anastomosis), hepaticojejunostomy, duodenojejunostomy. Estimated blood loss 650 mL; 1 unit packed red blood cells transfused. Operative time 5 hours 40 minutes. Jackson-Pratt drain placed near pancreaticojejunostomy.

At age 66 years, the surgical pathology from the Whipple resection specimen was reported. Final diagnosis: Moderately differentiated (grade 2) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head and uncinate process. Tumor size: 2.1 x 1.8 cm (significantly reduced from pre-treatment 3.8 cm). Extensive treatment effect: College of American Pathologists (CAP) tumor regression score 2 (moderate response; residual cancer with evident tumor regression). Margins: Pancreatic neck margin NEGATIVE (0.4 cm clearance). Common bile duct margin NEGATIVE. Posterior (retroperitoneal/SMA) margin NEGATIVE (0.1 cm - close but negative). Anterior surface NEGATIVE. Uncinate margin NEGATIVE (0.3 cm). All surgical margins negative (R0 resection). Lymph nodes: 0/22 lymph nodes positive for malignancy (ypN0). Lymphovascular invasion: present focally. Perineural invasion: present. Pathologic stage (post-neoadjuvant): ypT2 ypN0 M0, Stage IIA (AJCC 8th edition). Ampulla and duodenum: no tumor involvement. Proximal and distal bile duct margins: negative. Additional findings: Background chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) grade 1-2.

At age 66 years, on post-operative day 5 following the Whipple procedure, the patient was found to have biochemical leak from the pancreaticojejunostomy (drain amylase >3x upper limit of normal). This progressed to a clinically relevant grade B pancreatic fistula (International Study Group on Pancreatic Fistula - ISGPF definition) by post-operative day 7: drain amylase 4,800 U/L, drain output 180 mL/day, low-grade fever 38.1°C, no systemic sepsis. Management: Drain left in place, bowel rest with transition to total parenteral nutrition (TPN), octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneous TID initiated. Hospital stay extended to 18 days (standard 7-10 days). No radiologic or surgical intervention required. Fistula gradually resolved by post-operative week 5 with drain output decreasing and amylase normalizing. Patient discharged home on post-operative day 18 with drain still in situ; drain removed at 5-week post-operative clinic visit when output minimal.

At age 66 years, 6-week post-operative visit following Whipple procedure. Patient recovering well from grade B pancreatic fistula, which has resolved. Drain removed. Weight 3.5 kg below pre-operative weight. Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency confirmed (steatorrhea, elevated fecal elastase <200 mcg/g); pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) initiated with meals (pancrelipase 40,000 lipase units per meal). New-onset insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus post-Whipple; endocrinology co-management. CA 19-9: 48 U/mL (post-operative nadir). ECOG performance status 1. Pathology reviewed: R0 resection, ypT2N0M0, CAP regression score 2. Adjuvant therapy discussion: Plan to initiate adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen) once patient recovered. Target start approximately 8-10 weeks post-operatively. Cancer status: No cancer identified (R0 resection, pathologically node-negative).

At age 66 years (approximately 10 weeks post-Whipple, 11 months after initial diagnosis), the patient initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine plus capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen). Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle; capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21 of each cycle. Planned for 6 cycles (6 months). Treatment intent: adjuvant, curative intent. Baseline post-surgical CA 19-9: 48 U/mL. ECOG 1.

At age 66 years, during adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (cycles 1-3), the patient developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (HFS; palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) from capecitabine, requiring capecitabine dose reduction to 75% (from 1660 mg/m2/day to 1250 mg/m2/day). Grade 2 fatigue persisted. Grade 1 myelosuppression (neutrophil nadir 1.2 x10^9/L, no febrile neutropenia). Pancreatic fistula-related sequela: Fluid collection near the pancreatic anastomosis identified on routine imaging (2.8 cm simple-appearing fluid collection), monitored conservatively without intervention. No signs of infection. Hand-foot syndrome improved to grade 1 after dose reduction.

At age 66 years (approximately 3 months post-operatively, during adjuvant therapy cycle 3), the patient had surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast. Findings: Post-surgical changes from Whipple procedure. Pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis intact. Small fluid collection adjacent to anastomosis measuring 2.8 cm (decreased from prior 3.2 cm on prior scan); simple in appearance, no internal debris or gas. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal nodularity. No enlarged lymph nodes. Surgical bed without evidence of local recurrence. No ascites. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules. Impression: Expected post-operative changes. Decreasing anastomotic fluid collection, likely post-surgical seroma/resolving fistula-related collection. No evidence of disease recurrence. CA 19-9: 32 U/mL.

At age 66 years, clinical assessment during adjuvant chemotherapy (after cycle 4 of 6 gemcitabine/capecitabine). Patient tolerating reduced-dose capecitabine well. CA 19-9 declining: 32 U/mL (down from 48 U/mL at surgery, 85 U/mL at end of neoadjuvant, 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis). Anastomotic fluid collection resolving. Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome with capecitabine dose reduction. ECOG performance status 1. Peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin exposure grade 1 (mild; stable, slowly improving). Weight stabilizing with PERT and dietary supplementation. Diabetes management: HbA1c 8.2% on insulin regimen. No evidence of disease recurrence on imaging or clinically. Cancer absent. Plan: Complete remaining 2 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. Transition to surveillance after cycle 6.

At age 66 years, during cycles 5-6 of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine, the patient developed grade 3 fatigue requiring a 1-week dose delay between cycles 5 and 6. Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome persisted (stable). Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets nadir 72 x10^9/L) leading to gemcitabine dose reduction to 75% for cycle 6. No bleeding events. Patient completed cycle 6 with dose modifications. Total adjuvant course completed.

At age 66 years (approximately 7 months post-operatively, 1 month after completing adjuvant chemotherapy), the patient had end-of-treatment restaging CT abdomen/pelvis and CT chest. Findings: Post-Whipple anatomy. No anastomotic fluid collection identified (resolved). No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal disease. No enlarged lymph nodes. Surgical bed: expected post-operative fibrosis without mass lesion or nodular enhancement suspicious for recurrence. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules or masses. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Complete resolution of post-operative fluid collection. CA 19-9: 21 U/mL (within normal range <37 U/mL). CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (normalized from 8.4 at diagnosis).

At age 66 years, end-of-treatment assessment following completion of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. Patient has completed full multimodality treatment course: 8 cycles neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX, consolidative chemoradiation (50.4 Gy with capecitabine), R0 Whipple procedure (ypT2N0M0, CAP score 2), and 6 cycles adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. CA 19-9 now 21 U/mL (normal). No radiographic evidence of disease. ECOG performance status 1. Ongoing issues: mild residual peripheral neuropathy (grade 1, stable), pancreatic exocrine insufficiency managed with PERT, post-Whipple insulin-dependent diabetes. Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant noted; counseled on implications for family members. Transition to surveillance: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and CA 19-9 every 3-4 months for first 2 years, then every 6 months for years 3-5. Oncology follow-up every 3 months. Referred to cancer survivorship program. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease.

scenario_blurb: Locally advanced pancreatic adenocarcinoma (borderline resectable) in a 66-year-old with tumor abutting the SMA and SMV. CA 19-9 elevated to 1200. Treated with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles with CA 19-9 decline to 85 and partial response on imaging. Then chemoradiation with capecitabine. Successfully undergoes Whipple procedure with negative margins. Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine complicated by pancreatic fistula.
